title: Timeline of free and open-source software
rows: Compiler toolkit , started at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign . Initially a research project and known as Low-Level Virtual Machine
Adopted by Apple as their primary compilation platform for Mac OS X
Founded to support free software and oppose software patents in Europe
Formerly proprietary software , released as open source in 2002 after a crowdfunding campaign
There was no name for the project , until the Wikimedia Foundation was announced in June 2003 , when name MediaWiki was coined by a Wikipedia contributor
Integral to the development of Wikipedia

New Zealand Open Source Society ( NZOSS ) , a non-profit organization and incorporated society began with a suggestive letter by David Lane to the government , along with 400 supporters signatures to begin the advancement of open software in New Zealand

Descended from the Mozilla Application Suite , the project started as an experimental branch of the Mozilla Project . Originally titled Phoenix , then renamed as Firebird , the project was finally named Mozilla Firefox . The version 1.0 was released on November 9 , 2004
Second most popular web browser in the world
Created by Linux founder Linus Torvalds
World 's most popular distributed revision control system
Forms the majority of the code in Google Chrome , the most popular web browser in the world
Most popular mobile platform in the world
Has since enjoyed popular use in types of devices known as Chromebooks and Chromeboxes

description: This table provides a timeline of significant events and achievements related to free and open-source software projects. It includes details about the date of the event, the project involved, a description of the event, and notable achievements associated with it. The table serves as a historical reference for developments in the open-source software domain.
